body.text:
Attention, Wound, Laughter, and Measure: A Philosophical
Study of the Poetry of Ernesto de Montisalbi
Nico F. Declercq1
1 Georgia Institute of Technology, George W. Woodruff School of Mechanical
Engineering, Atlanta, GA, USA

Abstract
This article presents aphilosophical and literary study of the seven poetry collections
published under the pseudonym Ernesto de Montisalbi: Heart Embers, Soul Sparks, Light
Whispers, Contemplation, Measure, Dauwzucht, and Kingdom. Preserved together with companion podcasts within the Zenodo open-access repository, the collections are examined not
merely as isolated poetic works, but as acoherent multilingual corpus concerned withexistential observation, moral attention, memory, grief, humor, spirituality, institutional critique,
science, ordinary life, and cultural continuity.
The study argues that the corpus functions as asustained poetic practice of disciplined
attentiveness toward human existence in its emotional, social, historical, and philosophical dimensions. Across domestic interiors, hospitals, classrooms, cafés, train stations, landscapes,
bureaucracies, churches, universities, and ordinary daily routines, the poems repeatedly investigate mortality, family rupture, loneliness, parental alienation, institutional blindness,
moral injury, historical repetition, aging, vulnerability, and the fragility of interpersonal
continuity. At the same time, the collections consistently preserve beauty, affection, gratitude, friendship, mentorship, humor, travel, music, ordinary pleasure, and moments of quiet
wonder as essential counterweights to despair.
Particular attention is devoted to the corpus’sdistinctive synthesis of philosophical reflection, scientific imagery, literary psychology, regional observation, spiritual ambiguity, and
satirical social diagnosis. The article examines how concepts associated with measurement,
entropy, causality, institutional systems, and scientific attentiveness become poetic instruments for reflecting upon human dignity, mortality, moral responsibility, and the limits of
quantification. Simultaneously, the multilingual structure of the collections, spanning English, Dutch, and occasional French expression, is analyzed as avehicle for preserving regional
texture, cultural memory, and layered forms of belonging across Flanders, Belgium, France,
the United States, Sri Lanka, and broader international settings.
The article further investigates the companion podcasts as interpretive and archival artifacts that extend the philosophical accessibility of the corpus through oral reflection and
thematic contextualization. These recordings preserve notonly explanatory commentary
surrounding the poems, but also aliving interpretive atmosphere documenting how the collections understand themselves conceptually and emotionally within the archive.
The study concludes that the seven collections together form acoherent philosophicalpoetic archive of human attention. Rather than reducing existence either to pessimism or
naïve optimism, the corpus sustains acomplex equilibrium between suffering and gratitude,

1 2
grief and laughter, institutional critique and affection, spiritual uncertainty and reverence,
historical fragility and ordinary beauty. Positioned at the intersection of existential literature,
philosophical poetry, literary psychology, everyday-life poetics, satire, and cultural preservation, the work of Ernesto de Montisalbi contributes adistinctive contemporary meditation
on what remains worthy of observation, remembrance, and human care within modern life.
Finally, the present article constitutes notonly an analytical study of the literary and
philosophical works published under the pseudonym Ernesto de Montisalbi, but also arare
reflective exercise in which the author examines, from the perspective of ascientist and
scholar, the existential, emotional, and philosophical dimensions of his own literary alter ego;
the author therefore acknowledges that achieving abalanced presentation between critical
distance and personal proximity was among the most difficult aspects of this work, and
respectfully invites readers to regard this article primarily as an open gateway to the original
collections and companion materials themselves, upon which independent interpretation and
judgment should ultimately be formed. Keywords: Ernesto de Montisalbi; Nico F. Declercq; poetry; philosophical poetry; existential
poetry; literary psychology; grief; humor; ordinary life; institutional critique; spirituality; science
and poetry; memory; open-access preservation; Zenodo. 1 Introduction

The seven poetry collections published under the pseudonym Ernesto de Montisalbi — Heart
Embers, Soul Sparks, Light Whispers, Contemplation, Measure, Dauwzucht, and Kingdom —
together constitute abroad philosophical-literary project concerned with the observation of
human existence in its emotional, moral, social, spiritual, and historical dimensions. Although
each collection possesses its own atmosphere, stylistic rhythm, and thematic emphasis, the corpus
as awhole reveals acoherent intellectual orientation grounded in attention, memory, existential
reflection, and the persistent attempt to understand the fragile conditions under which human
beings live together. The collections were written over many years and emerge from awide
variety of lived environments: universities, hospitals, cafés, landscapes, train stations, churches,
kitchens, cities, lecture halls, roads, classrooms, family gatherings, cemeteries, waiting rooms,
and ordinary domestic interiors. Rather than presenting asingle continuous narrative, the
poems form an accumulated archive of observations, encounters, reflections, emotional reactions,
philosophical tensions, and moral impressions. The poetic voice moves continuously between
the intimate and the civilizational, between fleeting everyday moments and broader questions
concerning truth, dignity, memory, justice, responsibility, suffering, love, mortality, institutional
power, and historical decline.
Importantly, the corpus should not be understood merely as acollection of occasional emotional expressions or autobiographical fragments. The poems consistently operate at abroader
philosophical level. Everyday events become existential case studies. Small gestures acquire
moral significance. Humor becomes epistemological critique. Grief becomes ontological reflection. Ordinary routines become meditations on time, repetition, loneliness, affection, and
impermanence. The poems repeatedly transform seemingly minor observations into broader
inquiries concerning what it means to remain human within increasingly fragmented modern
societies.
© Nico F. Declercq

3
This orientation is explicitly reflected within the collections themselves. The author repeatedly emphasizes that the poems originate not from formal literary ambition alone, but from
observation, lived experience, thought, memory, and reflection upon humanity and the world.
The prologues consistently stress that the focus lies less on technical virtuosity than on the ideas,
emotional resonances, and contemplative states giving rise to the verses. The poems therefore
emerge as instruments of philosophical attention rather than merely aesthetic exercises. Adefining characteristic of the corpus is its refusal to reduce human existence to asingle
emotional register. Although many poems confront grief, death, alienation, betrayal, imprisonment, institutional cruelty, loneliness, moral injury, or historical violence, the collections are
equally permeated by humor, tenderness, absurdity, gratitude, wonder, affection, mentorship,
friendship, beauty, and quiet joy. The poetic universe of Ernesto de Montisalbi is therefore
neither nihilistic nor sentimentally optimistic. Rather, it insists upon the coexistence of darkness and light within ordinary human existence. This balance is crucial to the philosophical
integrity of the works. The corpus repeatedly suggests that suffering becomes psychologically
and morally dangerous when detached from humor, affection, beauty, or gratitude. Conversely,
joy becomes superficial when it refuses acknowledgmentof mortality, grief, injustice, or existential fragility. The poems therefore seek equilibrium between emotional honesty and existential
perseverance. Afuneral may stand beside acomic bureaucratic scene; ameditation on institutional collapse may be followed by reflections on wine, animals, children, music, or simple daily
rituals. The resulting literary landscape resembles lived reality itself: unstable, contradictory,
painful, beautiful, absurd, and intermittently transcendent.
Humor occupies an especially important position within this balance. Many poems employ irony, satire, exaggeration, or absurd juxtaposition not merely for entertainment, but as
mechanisms of philosophical clarification. Bureaucratic rigidity, academic vanity, ideological
blindness, social conformity, technological obsession, and institutional hypocrisy are frequently
exposed through comic inversion. Yet the humor rarely becomes cruel or cynical. Instead, it
functions as aprotective counterweight preventing existential reflection from collapsing entirely
into despair. Laughter becomes aform of cognitive relief through which painful truths can be
approached without psychological paralysis.
Another defining feature of the corpus is its extraordinary thematic range. The poems
move fluidly between intimate family scenes and large historical reflections; between physics and
prayer; between local Flemish dialect and cosmopolitan travel; between memories of childhood
and meditations on civilization; between erotic playfulness and metaphysical grief; between social
critique and profound gratitude toward mentors, parents, children, colleagues, workers, teachers,
and forgotten ordinary individuals. This breadth gives the collections an almost encyclopedic
human texture in which no single dimension of existence entirely dominates the others.
The corpus is also remarkable for the way in which scientific, philosophical, and literary
sensibilities intersect throughout the poems. Concepts associated with physics, mathematics,
entropy, causality, temporality, and measurement frequently appear within the verses, not as
technical demonstrations, but as metaphors for human limitation, institutional distortion, mortality, and existential order. The author’sscientific background therefore does not suppress
emotional depth; rather, it provides additional conceptual language through which everyday life
© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026

4
can be interpreted. Scientific thought becomes integrated into broader human reflection without
reducing human experience to mechanistic explanation.
At the same time, spirituality permeates much of the corpus in subtle and complex ways.
Churches, saints, prayer, biblical imagery, sacred rituals, and divine encounters appear repeatedly throughout the collections. Yet the poetry avoids simplistic doctrinal certainty. Faith is
presented less as ideological possession than as longing, humility, questioning, reverence, gratitude, or quiet confrontation with mystery. The sacred often emerges not within institutional
grandeur, but within beggars, elderly individuals, workers, children, forgotten persons, and moments of unexpected tenderness.
The multilingual and geographically diverse character of the corpus further reinforces its
broader human orientation. Poems appear in English, Dutch, and French, while settings range
across Belgium, France, the United States, Central Europe, Asia, and other locations. Yet
despite this geographical breadth, the poetry remains strongly grounded in concrete human
experience. Places are never merely decorative backgrounds; they function as emotional and
historical containers carrying memory, atmosphere, loss, and identity.
The present article accompanies the preservation of the seven collections and their associated podcasts within the Zenodo open-access repository. Its purpose is not promotional, but
analytical, philosophical, and archival. The article aims to provide future readers, researchers,
literary scholars, psychologists, philosophers, historians, and interdisciplinary investigators with
astructured interpretive framework through which the corpus may be approached as acoherent
philosophical-literary project rather than merely adispersed setof independent poems.
The inclusion of companion podcasts within the archive significantly expands this interpretive
framework. These podcasts function as educational and analytical companion artifacts that
help clarify thematic structures, philosophical tensions, symbolic motifs, historical references,
and emotional undercurrents present throughout the collections. Their conversational format
additionally provides an accessible interpretive bridge for future audiences unfamiliar with the
multilingual, interdisciplinary, and philosophically dense nature of the corpus.
Preserving the collections and podcasts within an open-access scholarly repository also reflects abroader commitment to long-term cultural accessibility. The archive ensures that these
materials remain freely available beyond commercial cycles, institutional restrictions, or technological ephemerality. In doing so, the project contributes to the preservation of acontemporary
multilingual poetic corpus that attempts to document notonly suffering and collapse, but equally
humor, resilience, beauty, gratitude, mentorship, affection, and the fragile dignity of ordinary
human life.
Ultimately, the poetry of Ernesto de Montisalbi may best be understood as an extended
discipline of attention directed toward the human condition itself. The poems observe without
fully condemning, mourn without surrendering entirely to despair, laugh without trivializing
pain, and contemplate existence without pretending to resolve its deepest contradictions. Together, the seven collections form asustained poetic investigation into what remains meaningful,
beautiful, absurd, painful, sacred, and worthy of remembrance within the unstable landscape of
modern human existence.
© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026 5 2 Corpus and Companion Materials 2.1 Primary Poetry Collections

The present study examines seven interconnected poetry collections authored under the pseudonym
Ernesto de Montisalbi: Heart Embers, Soul Sparks, Light Whispers, Contemplation, Measure,
Dauwzucht, and Kingdom. Although each collection possesses adistinct atmosphere, linguistic
texture, and thematic emphasis, together they form acoherent philosophical-poetic corpus concerned with memory, morality, grief, observation, humor, social critique, spirituality, historical
consciousness, and the fragile dignity of ordinary human life.
The collections are multilingual and interdisciplinary in character, moving between English,
Dutch, and occasional French expressions while drawing simultaneously from literature, philosophy, science, religion, history, and lived experience. Their diversity is not accidental. Rather,
the corpus progressively expands from intimate emotional observation toward broader meditations on institutions, civilizations, morality, historical repetition, and existential continuity.
Throughout the collections, the poetic voice remains grounded in attention: attention to people, routines, gestures, landscapes, failures, absurdities, injustices, and moments of unexpected
beauty.
Heart Embers occupies an especially intimate and emotionally vulnerable position within
the corpus. The collection is primarily concerned with memory, affection, family bonds, grief,
tenderness, emotional endurance, and the lingering warmth left behind after love, loss, or separation. The poems frequently revolve around domestic scenes, interpersonal relationships,
recollection, and emotional traces surviving beyond rupture or mortality. Fire imagery, warmth,
ashes, fading light, and lingering glow recur symbolically throughout the collection, reinforcing
the central ideathat human attachment continues to shape consciousness long after physical
presence has disappeared.
Stylistically, Heart Embers combines emotional immediacy with reflective restraint. The poems rarely collapse into overt sentimentality; instead, they derive much of their emotional force
from understatement, memory fragments, and quiet gestures. The collection repeatedly emphasizes the moral and existential importance of small acts of care, ordinary intimacy, parental
responsibility, and interpersonal loyalty. Suffering is present throughout the volume, yet it
remains balanced by gratitude, affection, humor, and moments of fragile human beauty.
Soul Sparks develops amore intense emotional and philosophical atmosphere. Whereas Heart
Embers often lingers within remembrance and tenderness, Soul Sparks focuses more heavily upon
sudden moral shocks, emotional combustion, existential disturbance, grief, outrage, confrontation, and resilience. Many poems in the collection are structured around moments of abrupt
realization in which ordinary reality suddenly reveals deeper ethical, psychological, or existential
tensions. The title itself is significant. The poems repeatedly suggest that human consciousness
is shaped through flashes of emotional ignition: brief but transformative encounters with suffering, injustice, absurdity, beauty, humiliation, courage, or love. The collection therefore possesses
amore volatile emotional rhythm than some of the later works. Nevertheless, even its darker
poems are frequently counterbalanced by irony, humor, compassion, or moments of stubborn
perseverance that prevent the atmosphere from becoming wholly nihilistic.
© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026

6
Light Whispers introduces amore contemplative and delicate poetic mode centered upon
fleeting beauty, ordinary wonder, spiritual suggestion, and the quiet textures of everyday existence. The poems often focus on transient impressions: light falling across aroom, silence
within landscapes, passing conversations, seasonal changes, animals, travel, cafés, fleeting human encounters, or moments of sudden stillness interrupting ordinary routine. The collection is
especially important for understanding the broader emotional balance of the corpus. Whereas
other volumes frequently confront grief, institutional cruelty, or existential instability directly,
Light Whispers demonstrates the author’sequal fascination with modest joy, peaceful observation, humor, gratitude, and subtle forms of transcendence embedded within ordinary life.
Beauty here is rarely grandiose. Instead, it appears quietly, often through understated moments
requiring attentiveness rather than spectacle.
Contemplation occupies one of the most inward and spiritually restrained positions within
the corpus. The collection repeatedly returns to silence, prayer, mortality, unresolved longing,
ethical responsibility, spiritual uncertainty, grief, restraint, and the fragile search for grace within
imperfect existence. Churches, saints, sacred imagery, memory, illness, aging, and existential
thresholds appear throughout the volume, though rarely in doctrinal or triumphalist form. The
poems in Contemplation often resist closure. Questions remain unresolved. Suffering is neither denied nor simplistically redeemed. Instead, the collection develops apoetic atmosphere
of humility before mystery. The sacred emerges less through certainty than through attention,
vulnerability, endurance, and the recognition of human limitation. This inward spiritual seriousness gives the collection ameditative quality distinct from the sharper satirical or institutional
critiques appearing elsewhere in the corpus.
Measure represents perhaps the most explicitly interdisciplinary collection within the archive.
The volume repeatedly explores the relationship between science, mathematics, institutional
judgment, human worth, mentorship, academic culture, measurement, truth, and the tension between quantitative systems and lived human reality. Physics, mathematics, cosmology, causality,
entropy, and scientific imagery appear throughout the collection, not as technical exposition, but
as philosophical metaphors through which broader questions concerning humanity, morality, and
civilization are examined. At the same time, Measure contains some of the corpus’ssharpest
institutional critiques. Universities, bureaucracies, academic metrics, conformity, intellectual
cowardice, and systems of reward are repeatedly examined through irony, moral reflection, and
existential concern. Yet the collection also contains warmth, mentorship, admiration for genuine
scholarship, and respect for intellectual discipline. The central philosophical question running
throughout the volume concerns what modern societies choose to value, count, reward, neglect,
or erase.
Dauwzucht, written primarily in Dutch, introduces agrounded, regional, and deeply observational poetic atmosphere strongly connected to everyday life, Flemish culture, work, travel,
domestic spaces, landscapes, cafés, local humor, ordinary professions, and human nearness. The
title itself evokes moisture, breath, mist, condensation, and fragile atmospheric presence, reflecting the collection’srecurring focus upon subtle emotional textures and transient moments
of lived reality. The Dutch-language character of the collection is philosophically significant.
The poems often adopt amore direct, earthy, conversational, and regionally textured tone than
© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026

7
the English-language collections. Workers, fishermen, farmers, civil servants, train stations, local
cafés, elderly figures, animals, and ordinary family interactions become central poetic subjects.
Yet beneath this grounded realism lies apersistent attentiveness to mortality, dignity, loneliness,
memory, humor, and social fragility. The poems repeatedly suggest that profound existential
insight may arise precisely from ordinary situations frequently overlooked by grand historical
narratives.
Finally, Kingdom expands the poetic horizon toward institutions, rulers, crowds, ideology,
bureaucracy, historical repetition, social ritual, collective blindness, and the cyclical instability
of civilizations. The collection examines power not merely politically, but anthropologically
and existentially. Empires, bureaucracies, crowds, public performances, obedience, propaganda,
institutional decay, and historical amnesia recur throughout the volume.
Yet Kingdom avoids simplistic political polemic. The poems are rarely tied to specific contemporary events or ideological programs. Instead, the collection investigates recurring structures of human behavior: the seduction of authority, the fragility of truth, the repetition of
historical errors, the theatricality of leadership, and the vulnerability of societies to collective
illusion. Irony, satire, historical memory, and philosophical skepticism permeate the collection,
though often accompanied by quieter moments of mourning for lost dignity, forgotten labor, and
the gradual erosion of cultural continuity.
Taken together, the seven collections form aremarkably broad poetic archive in which personal grief, scientific reflection, spiritual longing, institutional critique, humor, tenderness, historical consciousness, and ordinary human observation coexist within aunified philosophical
sensibility. The corpus consistently resists reduction to any single literary category because its
central concern remains the complexity of human existence itself.

2.2 Accompanying Podcast Materials In addition to the seven primary poetry collections, the present archival project includes seven
companion podcast recordings intended to accompany and contextualize the literary-philosophical
material preserved within the Zenodo repository:
• Ernesto_de_Montisalbi_Contemplation.mp3
• Ernesto_de_Montisalbi_Dauwzucht.mp3
• Ernesto_de_Montisalbi_Heart_Embers.mp3
• Ernesto_de_Montisalbi_Kingdom.mp3
• Ernesto_de_Montisalbi_Light_Whispers.mp3
• Ernesto_de_Montisalbi_Measure.mp3
• Ernesto_de_Montisalbi_Soul_Sparks.mp3
These podcast materials function as interpretive companion artifacts rather than promotional
supplements. Their primary role is educational, analytical, philosophical, and archival. Through
oral discussion and reflective commentary, the podcasts provide contextual frameworks that
© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026

8
assist listeners and future readers in approaching the thematic density, emotional complexity,
symbolic structures, and interdisciplinary references embedded throughout the poetic corpus.
Aparticularly important function of the podcasts lies in their capacity to clarify the philosophical orientation of the collections. Many poems contain condensed existential reflections,
scientific metaphors, historical references, religious symbolism, satirical inversions, or multilingual nuances that may not immediately reveal their deeper conceptual structure during afirst
reading. The podcasts therefore operate as interpretive bridges between poetic compression and
philosophical exposition.
The podcast accompanying Heart Embers focuses especially on memory, family, affection,
grief, emotional endurance, and the persistence of attachment beyond loss. The discussion surrounding Soul Sparks emphasizes emotional intensity, moral confrontation, psychological rupture, resilience, and moments of existential ignition. The Light Whispers podcast highlights
attentiveness to ordinary beauty, quiet wonder, subtle humor, spirituality, and transient forms
of happiness often overlooked within modern life. The podcast dedicated to Contemplation
explores prayer, silence, mortality, humility, spiritual uncertainty, and inward reflection, while
the Measure discussion concentrates more heavily upon science, mathematics, academic institutions, mentorship, judgment, and the ethical implications of quantifying human value. The
Dauwzucht podcast provides particular attention to Dutch-language atmosphere, regional identity, work, ordinary gestures, and grounded daily observation. Finally, the Kingdom podcast
examines historical repetition, institutional performance, ideological ritual, collective blindness,
and the philosophical structures underlying political and civilizational instability.
The conversational and oral nature of the podcasts serves an important pedagogical purpose. Philosophical poetry often risks appearing inaccessible when encountered solely through
condensed written form. By discussing the collections in an analytical and reflective manner,
the podcasts create interpretive pathways through which emotionally difficult, multilingual, or
philosophically dense poems become more approachable without sacrificing complexity.
The podcasts also preserve an additional layer of intellectual history surrounding the collections themselves. Future researchers, literary scholars, philosophers, psychologists, or historians
examining the corpus will notonly have access to the poems as static written artifacts, but
equally to oral interpretive materials documenting how the works were conceptually framed,
emotionally understood, and philosophically contextualized at the time of their archival preservation. Within the Zenodo repository, the integration of literary texts and companion podcasts
therefore creates amultidimensional archival structure in which written poetry and oral philosophical commentary coexist. The podcasts form an integral componentof the preserved corpus
rather than merely secondary supplementary material. Together, the poems and podcasts establish abroad open-access cultural archive concerned with preserving notonly literary expression
itself, but also the reflective and interpretive frameworks through which that expression may
continue to remain meaningful for future generations.

© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026 9 3 Methodological Note: Reading the Poems as Philosophical
Observation

The present article approaches the poetry of Ernesto de Montisalbi primarily as aform of
philosophical observation rather than exclusively as an objectof formalist literary evaluation.
Although questions of rhythm, imagery, rhyme, tone, symbolism, and linguistic construction
remain relevant throughout the corpus, the analytical emphasis adopted here focuses principally
upon the poems as reflective acts of attention directed toward human existence, social behavior,
memory, morality, suffering, beauty, absurdity, and historical continuity.
This methodological orientation is important because the collections themselves repeatedly
define their own poetic ambitions in terms of observation, contemplation, reflection, and experiential attention rather than technical perfection or literary self-display. The prologues explicitly
describe the poems as emerging from years of lived experience, fragments, observations, routines,
intellectual curiosity, emotional reaction, and sustained engagement with uncertainty. The poetry is therefore presented less as ademonstration of formal virtuosity than as an attempt to
think, observe, and preserve moments of existential significance through literary form. The collections consistently suggest that poetry functions here as adisciplined mode of noticing. Many poems focus not upon extraordinary events, but upon details frequently neglected
within ordinary life: aconversation in a café, aworker repairing machinery, aforgotten elderly
person, atrain platform, institutional silence, an animal beside the road, acup of coffee, a
bureaucratic gesture, amemory of ateacher, or afleeting emotional shift within domestic space.
The philosophical significance of these observations lies precisely in their refusal to separate
existential depth from everyday reality.
Consequently, the analytical framework employed in this article does not primarily evaluate
the poems according tonarrow criteria associated with technical literary schools, avant-garde
experimentation, or academic poetics alone. Such criteria, while not irrelevant, would risk obscuring the broader intellectual and existential intentions of the corpus. The poems repeatedly
privilege clarity of perception, emotional resonance, moral reflection, and conceptual suggestiveness over purely ornamental linguistic complexity. Even when rhyme, meter, irony, or symbolic
construction are carefully employed, they generally remain subordinate to the larger actof
attentive reflection.
This orientation places the corpus within abroader philosophical-literary tradition in which
poetry functions as existential inquiry rather than merely aesthetic production. Comparable
tendencies may be identified in poets and writers who treat verse as amode of reflection upon
lived reality itself: not simply as language arranged beautifully, but as language attempting
to preserve, examine, or confront aspects of existence thatordinary discourse often overlooks.
Within such traditions, poetry becomes amethod of philosophical seeing.
Aparticularly significant feature of the present corpus is the coexistence of intellectual abstraction and concrete immediacy. The poems frequently move between philosophical concepts
and ordinary material details without establishing rigid boundaries between them. Physics,
mathematics, mortality, memory, institutions, religion, and historical violence coexist beside
kitchens, train stations, fishermen, schoolchildren, cafés, landscapes, humor, and domestic ritu-
© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026

10
als. This movement reinforces the methodological principle that philosophical meaning emerges
not apart from ordinary existence, but through sustained attention to it.
The article also approaches the poems hermeneutically rather than biographically. Although
emotional intensity, autobiographical atmosphere, and psychological immediacy permeate many
texts, the collections themselves explicitly define their content as artistic and fictionalized expression written under apseudonym and distinct from the author’sscientific and professional
identity.
This distinction is methodologically essential. The poems are therefore not interpreted here
as documentary testimony, factual confession, political declaration, institutional accusation, or
direct autobiographical evidence. Instead, the poetic voice is treated as aconstructed literary
personathrough which broader existential, moral, psychological, social, and philosophical tensions are explored. Even when poems clearly draw emotional energy from lived experience, their
significance within the corpus lies less in empirical factuality than in the universal structures of
human vulnerability, grief, absurdity, humor, dignity, longing, and moral attention they seek to
illuminate.
The use of the pseudonym Ernesto de Montisalbi further reinforces this interpretive framework. The pseudonym creates an intentional separation between scientific authorship and poetic
subjectivity, thereby allowing the literary corpus to explore ambiguity, emotional vulnerability,
irony, anger, tenderness, satire, spirituality, and existential contradiction without collapsing
those dimensions into the author’sprofessional identity. The poetic speaker therefore functions
not as atransparent autobiographical self, but as aphilosophical and literary voice shaped
through reflection, observation, memory, and imaginative reconstruction.
Another important methodological principle concerns emotional plurality. The corpus should
not be reduced to asingle interpretive mood such as pessimism, grief, social criticism, or melancholy. Although suffering, mortality, institutional failure, and emotional injury are undeniably
present throughout the collections, the poems equally contain joy, humor, erotic playfulness,
gratitude, mentorship, friendship, travel, wonder, absurdity, and quiet happiness. The article
therefore adopts an interpretive approach attentive to tonal balance and emotional coexistence
rather than privileging darkness alone.
This balance reflects adeeper philosophical orientation underlying the corpus itself. Human
existence is presented neither as pure tragedy nor as naïve optimism, but as acondition of
simultaneous vulnerability and beauty. Laughter appears beside mourning. Satire appears
beside prayer. Institutional critique appears beside admiration for ordinary decency and human
kindness. The poems repeatedly resist reductive emotional simplification in favor of amore
unstable yet more realistic existential complexity.
The methodological perspective adopted throughout this article consequently combines elements of existential interpretation, literary hermeneutics, moral philosophy, literary psychology,
and cultural analysis. The aim is neither to canonize nor merely to summarize the poems, but to
examine how the collections function collectively as asustained poetic practice of observing human life in its contradictions, fragilities, absurdities, violences, affections, and fleeting moments
of grace.
Ultimately, the poetry of Ernesto de Montisalbi is approached here as aliterary archive of

© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026 11
attention itself: attention directed toward people, institutions, grief, beauty, power, memory,
work, ritual, suffering, humor, landscapes, language, and the unstable continuity of human
existence. The poems seek less to resolve the human condition than to remain honestly present
before it. 4 The Poetic Persona: Scientist, Observer, Witness, and Jester 4.1 The Pseudonym and the Separation from Scientific Identity

One of the most intellectually significant aspects of the poetic corpus is its publication under
the pseudonym Ernesto de Montisalbi rather than under the author’sscientific name. This separation is not merely decorative or commercially motivated. Rather, it functions as adeliberate
philosophical and methodological boundary between twofundamentally different modes of engagement with reality: scientific inquiry on the one hand and subjective poetic exploration on
the other.
Scientific authorship generally operates under conditions of methodological precision, empirical accountability, formal rigor, reproducibility, and conceptual restraint. The scientific voice
seeks clarity, coherence, demonstration, and the minimization of ambiguity. Poetry, by contrast,
often depends precisely upon ambiguity, emotional plurality, symbolic resonance, irony, contradiction, memory, intuition, fragmentation, and unresolved existential tension. The pseudonym
therefore creates aprotected literary space within which emotional vulnerability, satire, grief,
absurdity, longing, tenderness, and moral ambiguity may be explored without collapsing these
dimensions into the expectations associated with scientific discourse.
This distinction becomes especially important given the breadth of emotional and philosophical material contained within the collections. The poems frequently move through sorrow,
frustration, institutional critique, existential exhaustion, humor, erotic playfulness, spiritual uncertainty, political skepticism, admiration, affection, and moments of profound vulnerability.
The pseudonym permits such emotional and tonal plurality without requiring that the poetic
voice be interpreted as identical to the author’sprofessional or scientific identity.
At the same time, the separation is not absolute. The poetic persona of Ernesto de Montisalbi
clearly retains traces of scientific attentiveness, analytical discipline, and intellectual curiosity.
The speaker often observes human behavior with almost experimental precision, noticing patterns of institutional absurdity, emotional contradiction, social ritual, or existential repetition
in ways resembling empirical observation. Yet unlike scientific discourse, the poems donot
seek reduction, proof, or definitive explanatory closure. Observation remains open-ended and
interpretive rather than conclusively demonstrative.
The pseudonym therefore serves adouble function. First, it protects the scientific domain
from confusion with literary subjectivity. Second, it protects the poetic domain from the restrictive expectations of scientific formalism. The result is aproductive tension in which the
poems may incorporate scientific metaphors, analytical habits, and intellectual structures while
nevertheless remaining fundamentally literary and existential in orientation.
The name Ernesto de Montisalbi itself contributes to this literary architecture. The pseudonym
possesses an intentionally cosmopolitan and slightly theatrical resonance, evoking continental

© Nico F. Declercq DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.20433900 May 22, 2026 12
literary traditions, philosophical wandering, aristocratic decline, pilgrimage, memory, exile, and
symbolic distance from immediate biography. The personathus functions not merely as amask
hiding identity, but as aconstructed literary consciousness through which the poems acquire
tonal coherence and philosophical atmosphere.
Importantly, the poetic persona should not be understood as afictional character in the
narrow narrative sense. Rather, Ernesto de Montisalbi functions as areflective voice situated
somewhere between observer, participant, witness, satirist, mourner, teacher, traveler, and philosophical commentator. The poems repeatedly move between intimacy and detachment, confession and irony, affection and critique. The pseudonymous voice permits these transitions to occur
fluidly without requiring stable autobiographical transparency. The use of aliterary persona also
situates the corpus within along intellectual tradition in which pseudonymity enables philosophical experimentation and existential exploration. From Kierkegaard’spseudonymous writings to
Fernando Pessoa’sheteronyms and numerous modern literary personae, pseudonyms have frequently served as mechanisms through which writers investigate dimensions of consciousness,
contradiction, vulnerability, and reflection difficult to sustain within asingular public identity.
Within the present corpus, the pseudonym ultimately reinforces one of the central philosophical principles underlying the poetry itself: namely, that human beings cannot be reduced

Kingdom expands the critique of institutions into abroader meditation on power, crowds, historical repetition, and social performance. The kingdom motif is not limited to monarchy. It
refers more broadly to any organized structure of authority: states, empires, bureaucracies, universities, churches, ideological movements, public rituals, and collective identities. The poems
